coredata.dc:title: A simplistic approach to green future with eco-friendly luminescent carbon dots and their application to fluorescent nano-sensor ‘turn-off’ probe for selective sensing of copper ions
coredata.dc:creator: Das, Poushali
Ganguly, Sayan
Bose, Madhuparna
Mondal, Subhadip
Das, Amit Kumar
Banerjee, Susanta
Das, Narayan Chandra
coredata.dc:description: Zero-dimensional fluorescent nanoparticles having specificity as molecular probe appears to be strategically balanced fluorescent nano-probes. In this work, purified lemon extract and l-arginine have been thermally coupled for the extremely acute detection of Cu2+ in aqueous medium. The Cu2+ ions may be captured by the amino groups on the surface of the nano-sensor to form cupric ammine complex resulting in quenched fluorescence via an inner filter effect. Our proposed nano-probe is N-doped carbon dots (NCDs) which are efficiently selective as fluorescent chemosensor due to enormous binding affinity towards Cu2+ in a wide range of concentration (0.05–300μM) within a few minutes.

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure e-component body mmlmath affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authlast primabst ref specialabst

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: FL sensing method of Cu2+ ions
Evaluation of NCDs based Cu2+ sensor in environmental samples
Establishment of the possible sensing mechanism

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image of the NCDs. (b) High-resolution TEM image of NCDs. Lines and arrows indicate the lattice plane distances. (c) Size distribution of the NCDs. (d) FTIR spectrum of the as-prepared NCDs (e) EDX data and mapping of the NCDs. (f–g) AFM images of NCDs, inset shows the height profile of the corresponding AFM image.

(a) XRD pattern of the NCDs. (b) UV–vis absorption spectrum of the NCDs. (c) Excitation and emission spectra of the as prepared NCDs, inset are photos of NCDs under sunlight (left) and irradiated by 365nm light (right).
(a) XPS survey spectrum. XPS high-resolution survey scan of (b) C1s (c) N1s (c) O1s region of NCDs.

(a) Excitation dependent spectra of the NCDs. (b) Temperature dependent FL spectra of the NCDs. (c) FL spectra of the NCDs at different amount of l-arginine. (d) Concentration dependent spectra of the aqueous dispersion of the NCDs at λex=340nm.
Plausible mechanism of the formation of NCDs.

(a) FL spectra of NCDs in the presence of different Cu2+ concentrations (from top to bottom: 0, 0.05, 0.25, 0.5, 2, 4, 8, 10, 15, 20, 50, 100, 200 and 300μM) at λex =340nm. (b) The dependence of FL intensity on the concentration of Cu2+ ions within the range of 0–300μM, inset shows the linear relationship between the FL intensity and Cu2+ concentration, F 0 and F are FL intensities at 490nm in the absence and presence of metal ions respectively. (c) FL response of the NCDs in the presence (red) and absence (blue) of 15μM Cu2+ ions at various pH values. (d) The difference in FL intensity of NCDs dispersion containing different metal ions excited at 340nm in pH4 PBS solution. ([Mn+]=100μM).

(a) NCDs-based Cu2+ sensor in determination of environmental water samples: FL response of NCDs in presence of river water containing different concentrations of Cu2+ (from top to bottom: 0, 1, 2, 4, 8, 10, 15, 20, 50 and 100μM) at λex =340nm. (b) Relationship between F/F 0 and the concentrations of Cu2+ of river water, inset shows the dependence of log F 0/F with the concentrations of Cu2+ ions where F0 and F refers to the FL intensity of NCDs in the absence and presence of Cu2+. All emission spectra were collected at 340nm excitation.
Schematic approach used to prepare NCDs and fluorescence quenching.

head.ce:title: A simplistic approach to green future with eco-friendly luminescent carbon dots and their application to fluorescent nano-sensor ‘turn-off’ probe for selective sensing of copper ions

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Nanoscience and Technology, Indian Institute of Technology, Kharagpur 721302, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Rubber Technology Centre, Indian Institute of Technology, Kharagpur 721302, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Biotechnology, Indian Institute of Technology, Kharagpur 721302, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Materials Science Centre, Indian Institute of Technology, Kharagpur 721302, India

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: School of Nanoscience and Technology, Indian Institute of Technology, Kharagpur 721302, India.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Zero-dimensional fluorescent nanoparticles having specificity as molecular probe appears to be strategically balanced fluorescent nano-probes. In this work, purified lemon extract and l-arginine have been thermally coupled for the extremely acute detection of Cu2+ in aqueous medium. The Cu2+ ions may be captured by the amino groups on the surface of the nano-sensor to form cupric ammine complex resulting in quenched fluorescence via an inner filter effect. Our proposed nano-probe is N-doped carbon dots (NCDs) which are efficiently selective as fluorescent chemosensor due to enormous binding affinity towards Cu2+ in a wide range of concentration (0.05–300μM) within a few minutes.

ce:section.ce:para: Fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) have drawn eyes of extensive notice of many researchers owing to their unique and tunable optical properties with their size, low toxicity, good biocompatibility, excellent water solubility and ease of functionalization, enhance photostability [1,2]. CDs, a new born member of carbonaceous nanomaterial were first reported in 2004 since the invention of Xu et al. during the separation of SWCNT by the arc-discharge method. They have considerably drawn a lot of attention for their interesting and unique advantages [3–9]. CDs exhibit numerous advantages compared to both fluorescent organic dyes and quantum dots, such as chemical stability, low photo bleaching, tunable luminescence emission, excellent biocompatibility [2] and blinking [10]. CDs (<10nm size) show excellent photoluminescence properties, low molecular weight and low toxicity, chemical inertness and a high degree of water solubility [11]. Recently, carbogenic dots have potential applications in the fields of bio-imaging [12–19], energy conservation [20], light-emitting devices [21], sensing [22–31], photocurrent response activity [32], and photocatalysis [33]. Sun and co-workers described the prospective of passivated CDs for two-photon fluorescence cells imaging [34] and used CDs for optical imaging in live mice [12]. CDs are mainly prepared by top-down and bottom-up approaches [35,36]. In the top-down process, they are synthesized through electro-oxidation [37], acid-assisted chemical oxidation [38], laser ablation [39] using lamp black or candle soot, graphite as the precursor. These strategies require complicated, low yield, less selectivity and utmost synthesis conditions [40]. However, by using bottom-up approach CDs can be facilely prepared via carbonization of precursors molecule (such as amino acid [41,42], polyethylene glycol [43,44] and polyethyleneimine [45]) and solvothermal synthesis [46]. However, the synthesis of nitrogen doped carbon nano dots can be classified into three categories according to the nitrogen sources, (1) from small organic molecules like l-DOPA, ammonium citrate, DMF, urea, amino acids; (2) from inorganic molecules like NaNH2, aqueous ammonia (3) from natural sources such as strawberry juice [47], grass [48], Bombyx mori silk [49]. NCDs have several applications of biosensing [50], gas capture like CO2 [51], oxygen reduction reaction [52] etc. In the present work, NCDs have been used to design a sensing system for selective detection of Cu2+ ions. Copper (Cu2+) is a key trace element to all living organisms as a dietary mineral because it is a vital component of the complex cytochrome c oxidase which is a respiratory enzyme [53]. At a level of about 1.4 to 2.1mg/kg of body weight of copper remains in human body [54]. The safe limit of copper in drinking water to be 1.3ppm (20μM) has been set up by the U.S. Environmental Protection Agency (EPA). In the normal group the average concentration of copper in blood is 100–150μgdL−1 (15.7–23.6μM). Copper is an important trace dietary at very low amounts (<0.9mg per day). However, short-time exposure to high levels of copper can be reason of gastrointestinal disturbance and long-time exposure can cause havoc damage to the kidneys and liver. For this reason, researchers have taken interest in developing novel nanoparticles low-cost, simple and green synthetic process for detection of heavy metal. Many modern techniques for copper screening like atomic fluorescence spectrometry, atomic emissions and absorption spectroscopy, inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry (ICP-MS) demands expensive instrumentation, complicated sample preparation and time-consuming processes. Apart from these techniques, chemosensors offer a unique strategy for easy, inexpensive, rapid and reliable tracking of metal ions in biological and environmental samples. Chemosensor displays remarkable change in magnetic, optical or electrical signal when it gets attach with particular guest counterpart. Apart from the other sensors, luminescence chemosensor has numerous unique advantages due to their specificity, sensitivity and rapid response time [55–57]. Herein, we report a facile one-step strategy to synthesize aqueous phase green luminescent NCDs from lemon juice which was used as carbon source and l-arginine which has nitrogen content. When Cu (II) was introduced to the aqueous solution of NCDs, the fluorescence of the NCDs solution was quenched (turn off) due to the electron transfer process. The amino group present at the surface of the NCDs can bind with Cu(II) ions to form cupric amine complex which results in great quenching of fluorescence via inner filter effect [58]. Based on the fluorescence change caused by Cu(II) ions, a sensing platform has been designed for specific and sensitive detection of Cu(II) ions, indicating unique applications in advanced chemistry.

Lemons were purchased from local market of Khargpur, India. l-Arginine were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich, Germany and used without any further purification. CuSO4·5H2O, HgO, FeSO4·7H2O, CaCl2·2H2O, CdCl2·H2O, AgNO3, BaCl2·2H2O, (CH3COO)2Zn2·H2O, (CH3COO)2Mg·4H2O, (CH3COO)2Pb·3H2O, and ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid (EDTA) were purchased from Merck, Germany. All other chemicals used were of reagent grade.

The N-doped CDs with mean size 3.5nm were prepared from lemon juice and l-arginine through thermal coupling. Lemons were cut into small pieces and fresh juice was squeezed out. The juice obtained was then centrifuged at 8000rpm for 10min followed by filtration of the supernatant to get pulp free lemon juice. 7mL of purified extract of lemon juice and 0.21g of l-arginine were dissolved in 10mL of deionized water. The aqueous solution was transferred to a Teflon-lined steel autoclave and heated at a constant temperature of 200°C for 3h. A pale yellowish product was obtained which was cooled down to room temperature. The product was then dissolved in 15mL of deionized water and the particles were separated through filtration. To know the best optimum temperature we have synthesized the fluorescent NCDs at different temperatures 180°C, 200°C, 220°C and 240°C. In order to obtain high efficient fluorescence, we have prepared the NCDs upon varying the l-arginine amount 0.05g, 0.1g and 0.2g respectively. Obtained NCD solutions were confirmed by fluorescence emission spectra and stored at 4°C for further characterization. The synthesis procedure is illustrated in Scheme 1 .

The Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectrum of the NCDs were obtained on a FTIR spectrophotometer (Perkin Elmer, model-Spectrum-2, Singapore) in the range of 500–4000cm−1 with resolution of 4cm−1 and 16 scans. UV–visible absorption spectrum of the prepared NCDs in aqueous dispersion was measured using a UV Spectrometer (PerkinElmer, model-2 Singapore, Lambda35). Fluorescence experiments were measured on a Fluoromax_4C_1052D_4312_FM spectrofluorometer in aqueous solution. The fluorescence lifetime were measured on a time-resolved fluorescence spectrometer FL3-P-TCSPC (Horiba Jobin Jvon, France). The microstructure and morphology of the NCDs were analyzed using a high resolution transmission electron microscope HRTEM (JEOL, Japan operating voltage 200kV with filament LaB6). The elemental composition and mapping of the prepared NCDs were investigated by energy dispersive X-ray study (EDX, INCA PentaFET ×3, Oxford Instrument UK). The interlayer spacing and amorphous nature of the NCDs were recorded by X-ray Diffractometer (PANalytical High Resolution XRD-I, PW 3040/60). The zeta potential was obtained on a Zetasizer Nano ZS90 (Malvern Instruments, Manchester, U.K.) Raman measurements were performed using spectrometer (MODEL T64000, Make JobinYvon Horiba, France). Argon-Krypton mixed ion gas laser (MODEL 2018 RM) was used as an excitation source. Thermoelectric cooled front illuminated 1024 256 CCD, MODEL Synapse TM (Make JobinYvon Horiba, France) was used as the detector. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was measured with a VG Scientific ESCALAB MK II spectrometer (UK) equipped with Mg Kα excitation source (1253.6eV) and a five-channeltron detection system. Using tetramethylsilane (TMS) as an internal standard 1H NMR was recorded in on a Bruker, 600MHz instrument at ambient temperature.

The morphology and structure of prepared NCDs were confirmed by HRTEM micrograph. As shown in Fig. 1a the NCDs are well dispersed without apparent aggregation and exhibited spherical shape. High-resolution TEM image (Fig. 1b) of NCDs show lattice plane distances. NCDs have a narrow size distribution ranging from 2.5–4.5nm with maximum population at about 3.5nm (Fig. 1c). The different functional groups and linkages on the NCDs were studied using FTIR spectroscopy (Fig. 1d). In the spectrum, peak at 3559cm−1 is assigned to OH or NH stretching vibrations. Peaks at 2939, 1699, 1590, 1398 and 1360cm−1 indicates the presence of –CH, CO, CC,–COO– and CO stretching respectively. Peaks observed at 1185cm−1 and 768cm−1 for C−N stretching vibrations and CH bending vibrations respectively. The presence of polar groups in the NCDs makes them hydrophilic and improves water dispersibility. The elemental composition of the CDs was estimated from EDX data and the distribution was recorded by mapping (Fig. 1e). The mass and atomic ratios of carbon (C), nitrogen (N) and oxygen (O) of the NCDs were found to be 51.18: 5.11: 43.70 and 57.92: 4.96: 37.12, respectively. The sufficient amount of oxygen present in the C-dots supports its high water tolerance character. The atomic force microscopy topography image of NCDs on silicon substrate was analyzed. The AFM image (Fig. 1f–g) shows that the NCDs were quasi-spherical and well-dispersed. The height profile of the NCDs obtained from AFM was also shown in inset of Fig. 1f.

A wide peak centered around 27.2° (002) in XRD pattern (Fig. 2a) indicates highly disordered carbon atoms and graphitic structure of the NCDs [59,60]. The UV–visible absorption spectrum of the carbon nanodots shows a maximum absorption at 215nm (Fig. 2b). This is assigned to the π-π* electronic transition of the CC bond. The aqueous solution of synthesized NCDs with an abundance of 3.5nm in size exhibit a broad emission spectra centered at 490nm when excited at 340nm which was shown in Fig. 2c (inset shows green luminescence of the aqueous dispersion of the NCDs). The intensity of the photoluminescence (PL) peak depends on the number of particles excited at a specific wavelength that means for NCDs maximum population of the particles are excited at 340nm. This indicates the fluorescent character of NCDs. The zeta potential was recorded to be −20.6mV which can be assigned to the highly stable anionic CDs containing nitrogen groups on the surface. Even after three months of storage in air, their color remains unchanged without any precipitated or floating particles and they were still luminescent. This is may be due to tiny particle size and electrostatic repulsions between the CDs. Further, we analyzed the effect of dissolved oxygen in the NCDs solution to get reliable analysis result because oxygen can itself acts as a molecular collisional quencher. 4mL of NCDs solution was purged by continuous flow of nitrogen gas for 25min. The fluorescence signal (Fig. S1 of the Supporting information) of the NCDs solution was almost unchanged before and after the removal of dissolved oxygen by nitrogen purging. Therefore, from the dissolved oxygen test, we can say that the effect of dissolved oxygen in the fluorescence activity can be neglected. The attempts to obtain the Raman spectra were unsuccessful because of the intense fluorescence property of the NCDs [61,62].

The XPS spectrum of the as-prepared NCDs gives evidence for the nitrogen doping surface state shown in Fig. 3a. The peaks at 531.6, 400.4 and 284.5eV are attributed to O1s, N1s and C1s, respectively. The high resolution C1s spectrum shows four peaks at binding energies of 288.4, 286.5, 285.4 and 284.5eV (Fig. 3b), which are assigned to OCO (sp2), CO (sp2), CN (sp3) and CC (sp3), respectively [63,64]. The N1s peaks at 401.1 and 400.3eV (Fig. 3c) are associated with nitrogen in the states of NH (sp3) and (C)3-N (sp3), respectively [48]. This indicates that the CDs are nitrogen-doped. The O1s signals at 533.5 and 531.6eV (Fig. 3d) can be assigned to oxygen in the form of COC/COH and CO, respectively [48]. The 1H NMR spectrum (Fig. S2 of the Supporting information) of the nanodots were performed in D2O which reveals signals in different chemical environments. Peaks in the region 1.4–3ppm for sp3 CH protons and amine groups of the protons, 3–4.5ppm for the protons attached with carbonyl, hydroxyl and ether groups [65].

One of the unique properties of the NCDs is its fluorescent character. The shift in the PL peak from 470 to 595nm was observed when excitation done ranging from 320 to 440nm, shown in the excitation dependent PL spectra (Fig. 4a). It is necessary to mention the reason of excitation dependent emission maxima and multi-exponential PL decay of CDs reported in most literature reports. Multiple species present (Fig. S3 of the Supporting information) in the system may interact with different energy traps which give rise to different absorption and emission from all of these species. Therefore for different excitation, contribution of the species will be different for emission. Hence emission of the CDs will depend on excitation wavelength. Furthermore, as different species are present, they will choose different paths for PL decay. The interaction of energy traps with the excited states of different species will give rise to different excited state decay paths. Hence, the PL decay curve is supposed to be multi-exponential in nature which supports our experimental data (Fig. S5 of the Supporting information). Moreover, the excitation dependent luminescence feature of the CDs depends largely on the nature of their surface present which is discussed elsewhere [65]. Due to the presence of several functionalities on the surface of the CDs, series of emissive traps between π and π* of CC may be obtained. On illumination at a specific excitation wavelength a surface energy trap of the CDs dominates the emission. Upon changing the excitation wavelength, other corresponding surface state emissive traps become governing. This observation indicates that the mechanism of PL is controlled by both the surface defects and size effects. The temperature dependent fluorescence spectra of the NCDs dispersion (Fig. 4b) shows increase in fluorescence signal as the temperature rises from 180°C to 200°C but the fluorescence (FL) intensity remains almost same at 220°C when excited at 340nm. It also shows a decrease in FL intensity at 240°C. This is because of the complete carbonization of the NCDs. This assigned that illumination at a certain wavelength affects the FL intensity with temperature. Further we have studied the dependence of l-arginine amount on the FL intensity of the NCDs. Fig. 4c shows by increasing the amount of l-arginine from 0.05g to 0.2g, keeping the volume of purified lemon extract as 7mL in each case, the FL intensity gradually increases. The FL intensity shows highest value at 0.2g l-arginine amount. Hence the optimized value for the preparation of NCDs was fixed at that ratio. It indicates that the FL emission intensity is primarily a function of the concentration of a certain fluorophore. From the Fig. 4d it can also be shown that the FL intensity of the NCDs increases upon increasing the concentration. The salt effect of NCDs on the fluorescent properties has been measured. The FL intensity increases sharply with increasing KCL concentration, when the KCl concentration is lower than 0.7mol/L (Fig. S4 of the Supporting information). However, the FL signal remains almost unchanged as the concentrations of the KCL rise from 0.7 to 2.0mol/L. This result indicates the fact that the aggregation of the NCDs can be effectively controlled by the ionic strengths of the salt and also individually separate NCDs under salt interference. The effect of the salt concentration on the FL intensity described that these CDs can be applicable to the extreme environmental conditions due to their strong luminescence property at higher salt concentration. The aqueous dispersion of the NCDs shows green luminescence on UV exposure at 365nm. According to Lei Wang et al., when carbon nanodots contain carboxyl groups and carbonyl groups on their surface, it will definitely lead to some common transient species in transient absorption spectroscopy [66]. From the recent findings on green luminescence carbon quantum dots via steady state photoluminescence analysis and HRTEM it was confirmed that green luminescence originated from disordered edge states [67]. Relying on the edge state of the carbon backbone which contains carbonyl groups and carboxyl groups, the generation of some special conformations can be accelerated by combination of carboxyl and carbonyl functionalities in the vicinity at the edge of the nano particles. The hybridization of the carbon backbone and these functionalities forms the common origin of green emission [66].

Using quinine sulfate in 0.1M H2SO4 (literature value of quantum yield 0.54 at 340nm) as a reference, the quantum yield value for the NCDs was found to be 7.7% which is higher than the reported value of carbon quantum dots [68]. The quantum yield was calculated using the following equation.
(1)
Q CD
=
Q R
×
I CD
/
I R
×
A R
/
A CD
×
η 2
CD
/
η 2
R
where ‘Q’ refers to the quantum yield, ‘I’ represents the intensity of luminescent spectra, ‘A’ is the absorbance at particular excited wavelength and ‘η’ is the refractive index of the solvent used. The subscript ‘CD’ refers to the carbon dots and ‘R’ refers to the reference in this equation. The FL lifetime of the NCDs were measured to be 10.6ns according to the FL decay profile (Fig. S5 of the Supporting information). The short lifetime of these NCDs indicate the radiative recombination of the excitons which give rise to FL [43,69].

In Fig. 5 the plausible mechanism of the formation of NCDs was schematically interpreted. The mechanism for the preparation of NCDs from purified lemon extract and l-arginine includes carbonization of its constituents. Hydrothermal treatment of sucrose ends up in a quite complicated material structure and a well-defined strategy has not been reported yet. Hydrothermal carbonization of sucrose undergoes hydrolysis for the formation of fructose and glucose. Isomerization of glucose yields in fructose. The decomposition and dehydration of fructose or glucose yields in different organic acids like acetic, propionic, lactic, levulinic and formic acids, phenols, aldehydes, ketones and various soluble products like furfural compounds (for example: furfural,5-methyl furfural,5-hydroxymethyl furfural). The hydronium ions generated from the above mentioned acids may act as catalyst in reaction stages of decomposition. It is necessary to mention that due to the existence of weak acid such as ascorbic acid and citric acid in lemon extract the decomposition and dehydration stages take place in controlled fashion. The intermolecular dehydration of these products with l-arginine give rise to several coupling compounds. The polymerization and condensation reaction ends up to form polymers. Aromatization and generation of aromatic clusters proceeds via cycloaddition reaction, aldol condensation, hydroxymethyl mediated furan resin condensation. In aqueous solution when the concentration of these cluster compounds reaches at critical supersaturation point then burst nucleation occurs and NCDs are formed [70].

ce:section.ce:section-title: FL sensing method of Cu2+ ions
ce:section.ce:para:
For sensitivity study, there should be a useful relationship between the quenched FL intensity and the Cu2+ concentration that can be chosen for quantitative detection of Cu2+. Different concentrations of Cu2+ solution ranging from 0 to 300μM were investigated. In a typical run, aqueous dispersion of NCDs (200μL) was added into 10mL calculated amount of various concentration of Cu2+ solution. The FL spectra were analyzed after reaction of 10min at room temperature. Fig. 6a shows the PL spectra of the NCDs at different concentration of Cu2+ solution, revealing that FL intensity is sensitive to Cu2+ concentration. The FL intensity of the NCDs gradually decreases with the increase of Cu2+ concentration when excited at 340nm. The time-dependent FL spectra (Fig. S6 of the Supporting information) of the NCDs-Cu2+ indicates that only 10min required for the completion of the reaction between Cu2+ and NCDs. The quenching mechanism of the NCDs can be realized more accurately by Stern-Volmer analysis. Quenching mechanism which is originated from collisional interaction between the quencher and the fluorophore is named as dynamic quenching. Dynamic quenching can be described by Stern-Volmer equation as follows [71].
(2)
F 0
/ F =
τ 0
/ τ = 1 +
k q
τ 0
Q
where, F 0 and F are the FL intensities in the absence and presence of the quencher, respectively. τ 0 and τ are the FL lifetime in the absence and presence of the quencher respectively. k q is the rate constant of dynamic (collisional) quenching and [Q] is the quencher concentration in solution. Another type of quenching, i.e. static quenching occurs when a non-fluorescent complex formed between the fluorophore and the quencher. For this type of quenching the Stern-Volmer equation can be described as follows.
(3)
F 0
/ F = 1 +
K SV
Q
where, K SV is the association constant. In static quenching the lifetime of the fluorophore is unperturbed and hence, τ 0/τ =1. Fig. 6b shows the dependence of F/F 0 on the concentration of the Cu2+ ions over the range 0–300μM. The plot does not fit a conventional linear Stern-Volmer equation which indicates both static and dynamic quenching processes occur in this sensor probe [72]. Fig. 6b inset shows the corresponding relationship between log F 0/F versus concentration of Cu2+ ions. A good linearity (R2 =0.998) can be obtained in the concentration range 0 to 15μM. The limit of detection (LOD) was calculated to be 0.047μM using the three times standard deviation rule (LOD=3σ/s, where σ is the standard deviation of the blank sample and s is the slope of the calibration curve). This detection limit (0.047μM) is lower than the other reported methods [73,74] and comparable to previously reported other alternative methods [75–77]. This limit also satisfactorily meets the safe limit of copper ions (~20μM) in drinking water set by the U.S. Environmental Protection Agency. The pH of the aqueous solution of the NCDs is another vital factor that can greatly affect the sensing probe of copper detection. The FL intensity of the NCDs in both the cases (in the absence and presence of Cu2+ ions) are pH-dependent (Fig. 6c). The NCDs (blue columns in Fig. 6c) exhibit strong FL intensity in the pH range 4 to 6. However, the FL intensity becomes weak at the pH value >9. In the presence of copper ions the quenched FL intensity can be observed over the pH range 2–12 (red columns in Fig. 6c). In strong acidic solution (pH value less than equal to 3) the amino groups present at the surface of the NCDs become protonated and are incapable of complexing with Cu2+ to form cupric ammine complex. In alkaline medium (pH value >7), the quenching activities does not meet to expectation, which may be the reason of partial hydrolysis of copper (Cu2+) ions in the alkaline media hinders the complexation reaction between the amino groups of the NCDs and copper ions. This reveals that in weakly acidic media (pH value 4 to 6) have high quenching efficiencies and are suitable for sensing of copper (Cu2+) ions.

For selectivity study of this sensing system, the effect of the FL intensity in the presence of respective metal ions including Cd2+, Ba2+, Hg2+, Fe2+, Ag+, Ca2+, Li+, Mg2+, Pb2+ and Zn2+ under the same conditions were investigated (Fig. 6d). In the spectra, no huge decrease in FL intensity was observed when the above metal ions were added in the aqueous dispersion of NCDs. In contrast, much lower FL was observed on addition of Cu2+ in the NCDs dispersion. The selectivity of the Cu2+ ions is due to the rapid chelation process with nitrogen and oxygen of the NCDs and higher thermodynamic affinity of the copper ions than other transition metal ions [48].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evaluation of NCDs based Cu2+ sensor in environmental samples
ce:section.ce:para: The outstanding selectivity and sensitivity of the NCDs to Cu2+ ions suggest that this nanosensor can be applied for detection of copper ions in environmental prospects. We applied a standard addition method to sense the concentration of Cu2+ ions in river water [58]. To evaluate the performance of this sensor probe in practical application, we conducted the investigation using water samples obtained from Ganga River, West Bengal, India. Before the analysis, the river water sample was filtered through 0.20μm membrane and centrifuged at 14000rpm for 20min. The obtained water samples were then spiked with different concentration of Cu2+ ions and then evaluate using the proposed mechanism. The FL intensity (excited at 340nm) of the NCDs in the presence of river water containing different concentration of Cu2+ ions dramatically decreases with increasing the concentration from 1 to 100μM as shown in Fig. 7a. The relationship between F/F 0 on the concentration of the Cu2+ ions over the range 0–100μM was shown in Fig. 7b. Inset (Fig. 7b) shows the dependence of log F 0/F versus concentration of Cu2+ ions (from 0 to 15μM). In spite of the hindrance of various organics and minerals in the river water, this sensing probe can still perform for the selective detection of Cu2+ ions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Establishment of the possible sensing mechanism
ce:section.ce:para: As mentioned above the decrease of FL signal may be due to the formation of cupric ammine complex at the surface of the NCDs. From the recent studies, the FL quenching (turn off) mechanism may be attributed to the inner filter effect, electron transfer process, non-radiative recombination pathway and ion binding interactions [78]. The PL spectra of the aqueous NCDs dispersion under various conditions (in absence and in the presence of 20μM copper ions) have been measured. From the spectra, (Fig. S7 of the Supporting information) it can be seen that the NCDs give strong peak at 490nm when excited at 340nm. Whereas, on the addition of Cu2+, the PL peak shows a definite decrease in intensity indicating that the Cu2+ ions can dramatically quench the FL of NCDs. Cu2+ is a paramagnetic ion with d9 system having one unfilled d shell and thus can quench the FL of the NCDs via energy or electron transfer [79]. It is believed that the chelation of Cu2+ with the nitrogen and oxygen at the surface of the NCDs bring them close in proximity leading to the substantial decrease in FL signal [80]. When a strong Cu2+ chelating agent, i.e. EDTA was added to the NCDs and Cu2+ solution the FL signal was entirely restored which indicates that the inner filter effect associated with the cupric ammine complex can be terminated with a competitive complexation reaction and as a result FL of the NCDs becomes reversible.

The as-prepared FL based NCDs and the precursor both are eco-friendly and easy to prepare. It shows many promising advantages including good sensitivity, high selectivity, and fast detection of Cu2+ ions and rarely interfered by other metal ions when excited at 340nm in aqueous dispersion. The FL intensity of the NCDs versus Cu2+ ions concentration shows good linearity from 0 to 15μM with a detection limit of 0.047μM. This result indicate that NCDs can be a selective sensing probe of environmental application for detection of Cu2+ ions in drinking water which has a safe limit of 20μM set up by the U.S. Environmental Protection Agency (EPA). For the excellent luminescence property of these CDs they can be a promising candidate for further potential applications in bio-imaging, LEDs, light emitting devices as directed to green future.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.msec.2017.03.045.
